comments.text:
A possible reformulation that may be worth recording. Setting $K = X^\kappa$ and $W_1(m) := \sum_{i=1}^K \omega_{>K}(m+i)$ for $m \in [X, 2X)$: Reduction. A bad block of length $2K$ at scale $X$ forces at least $K$ values of $m$ to satisfy $W_1(m) \ge K$. Markov on the fourth central moment gives $$ |{m : W_1(m) \ge K}| \le M_4(W_1)/(K-\mu)^4 $$ with $\mu = K\log(1/\kappa) + o(K)$ by Mertens. So $M_4(W_1) \ll XK^{2+o(1)}$ uniformly for $\kappa > 1/e$ would yield $k(n) \ll n^{1/e + o(1)}$. Pairing identity. Writing $W_1 = \sum_{p>K} I_p$ and $f_p = I_p - \lambda_p$, expanding $M_4 = \sum_m (\sum_p f_p)^4$: $$ M_4(W_1) = 3X \cdot \mathrm{Var}(W_1)^2 + R $$ where $R$ is the all-distinct + three-equal + four-equal cumulant residual. The three-equal and four-equal pieces are $O(XK)$ trivially; the all-distinct piece reduces to a level-4 sieve discrepancy bound (call it L6). Numerical certification. At $\kappa = 0.34$, across $X \in [10^5, 10^8]$: $M_4/(3X\sigma^4) = 1.00 \pm 0.02$. Kurtosis is exactly 3 over four decades. The standardized $W_1$ converges to standard Gaussian. The variance gap. The remaining content is $\mathrm{Var}(W_1) \ll K \log^B X$. Equivalently, with $T_{pq} = S_p 	imes S_q$ (Cartesian product of shifted intervals mod $p, q$) and $R_{pq}$ defined as the count of $m \in [X, 2X)$ with $m  mod pq \in T_{pq}$, minus $XK^2/(pq)$: $$ \left| \sum_{K < p 
e q \le 2X} R_{pq} ight| \ll XK \log^B X \quad (	ext{L5}) $$ Numerically (sampling 50K of 5M pairs at $X=10^4$, $K=22$): $\sqrt{\langle R_{pq}^2angle} \approx 2.8$ vs trivial bound $K^2 = 484$ – already a 200x per-pair improvement. But Cauchy-Schwarz on $\sum R_{pq}^2$ still overshoots the true sum by about 600x. The remaining cancellation is signed across pairs, not magnitudinal – so $L^2$ approaches cannot close L5; only direct bilinear estimates can. By Fourier expansion: $$ \sum_{p 
e q} R_{pq} = \sum_{p 
e q}  rac{1}{pq} \sum_{(a,b) 
e 0} A_p(a) , A_q(b) , E_X(a/p + b/q) $$ with $A_p(a) = \sum_{i=1}^K e(ai/p)$ and $E_X(	heta) = \sum_{m=X}^{2X-1} e(m	heta)$. The cancellation lives in the region where $|a/p + b/q|$ is small – additive collisions of fractions with prime denominators. Friedlander-Iwaniec dispersion (Opera de Cribro Ch. 17) appears to be the right tool. L6 is the same machinery at sieve dimension 4. Across $\kappa \in [0.40, 0.45]$, $\mathrm{Var}(W_1)/(K ho(1/\kappa)) \approx 1.04$ – the variance constant numerically equals the Dickman function, which is suggestive of a closed form I cannot identify. I want to be explicit: I do not claim a theorem improving on Erdos’s [Er76e] $k(n) \le \exp(-(\log n)^c) n^{1/2}$. The chain to $n^{1/e+o(1)}$ is rigorous modulo L5 and L6, both of which I can numerically support but cannot prove. I’d value any pointers to existing work that closes either. Two specific questions: 1. Has anyone applied the polynomial-savings Matomaki-Radziwill-Tao machinery to $f$ equal to the indicator of $K$-smooth integers? Its pretentious distance to 1 is $\sqrt{\log(1/\kappa)}$, so whether MRT gives unconditional polynomial savings here depends on the dependence on $\mathbb{D}(f, 1)$. 1. The Tao-Teravainen [TaTe25] / Lau [Lau26] machinery handles $\omega(n+k)$ for consecutive integers via Maynard sieve + Pilatte. Does anything in section 5 of TaTe25 (the variance bound for $\sum \omega(n+h)/2^h$) adapt to unweighted sums in our prime range? Full notes (Cauchy-Schwarz autopsy, full numerics, code) available on request. – Darin Dimitroff

I found that Erdős revisited this problem in [Er76e], in which he proved\[\log k(n) \gg \sqrt{\log n\log\log n},\]the same order of magnitude as Tang's bound (I haven't checked what constant Erdős argument gives). He also claims a proof of an upper bound of\[k(n) \leq \exp(-(\log n)^c)n^{1/2},\]but did not even know whether $k(n)\leq n^{1/2-c}$ for some constant $c>0$. These bounds may have been discovered after [Er65], and of course he may have revisited this problem in a later paper yet to be discovered.

I recently wrote a short note establishing an improved lower bound for $k(n)$:\[ k(n) \ge \exp\Bigl(\bigl(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - o(1)\bigr)\sqrt{\log n\,\log\log n}\Bigr). \]The proof relies on classical estimates for the distribution of smooth numbers. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Not sure what Erdős intended by the "trivial bound", but one can get $k(n) \leq (1+o(1)) n^{1/2}$ by observing that if $k \geq (1+\varepsilon) n^{1/2}$ and $n$ is large then one of $m+1,\dots,m+k$ will be divisible by a prime $n^{1/2} < p < (1+\varepsilon) n^{1/2}$, and hence not divisible by a prime larger than $k$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
